title: Git & GitHub
content:
## 🤔 Have You Ever…

* Lost work after hours of effort?
* Overwritten the wrong file version?
* Had files like:
  `project-final.html`
  `project-final-final.html`
  `project-final-FINAL2.html` 😩

👉 These problems happen because there is **no version control**.

---

## 🧠 What Problem Does Git Solve?

Imagine writing a book.

Without Git:

* You only have the **latest copy**
* Mistakes can’t easily be undone
* Working with others causes confusion

With Git:

* Every change is **saved in history**
* You can return to **any previous version**
* Multiple people can work **without breaking each other's work**

📌 **Git is like a time machine + backup system for your files.**

---

## 🗂 Real-Life Analogy

| Real Life                              | With Git     |
| -------------------------------------- | ------------ |
| Saving multiple versions of a document | Commits      |
| Undo history                           | Git log      |
| Google Docs version history            | Git timeline |
| Cloud backup                           | GitHub       |

---

## 🔍 Git vs GitHub

| Git                             | GitHub                 |
| ------------------------------- | ---------------------- |
| Tool installed on your computer | Website                |
| Tracks file changes             | Stores projects online |
| Works offline                   | Requires internet      |
| Saves versions                  | Shares versions        |

📌 **Git = Version control tool**
📌 **GitHub = Cloud storage for Git projects**

---

# 🛠 Core Git Commands

---

## 1️⃣ `git init` — Start Tracking a Project

```bash
git init
```

Begins version control in your folder.

---

## 2️⃣ `git add` — Prepare Files to Save

```bash
git add .
```

Moves changes to the **staging area**.

🧠 Like selecting photos before uploading.

---

## 3️⃣ `git commit` — Save a Version

```bash
git commit -m "Created initial files"
```

A **commit** is a saved snapshot of your project.

---

## 4️⃣ `git log` — View History

```bash
git log
```

Shows all previous saved versions.

---

## ☁️ 5️⃣ `git push` — Upload to GitHub

```bash
git push origin main
```

Uploads your saved versions to GitHub.

### Why `origin main` Matters

| Part     | Meaning                     |
| -------- | --------------------------- |
| `origin` | Your GitHub repository      |
| `main`   | The branch you are updating |

---

## 🔄 6️⃣ `git pull` — Download Updates

```bash
git pull origin main
```

Gets the latest changes from GitHub.

🧠 Like refreshing a shared document before editing.

---

# 🧪 Practice Project: File Versioning

This project helps you practice Git **without needing HTML yet**.

---

## 📂 Step 1: Create Project Folder

```bash
mkdir my-project
cd my-project
git init
```

---

## 🔗 Step 2: Connect to GitHub

Create an empty repository on GitHub named **my-project**, then run:

```bash
git remote add origin https://github.com/yourusername/my-project.git
git branch -M main
git push -u origin main
```

---

## 📝 Step 3: Create Files

```bash
touch notes.txt tasks.txt ideas.txt
```

Add content:

```bash
echo "First note" > notes.txt
echo "Task 1: Learn Git" >> tasks.txt
cat > ideas.txt
```

Press **CTRL + D** after typing inside `ideas.txt`.

---

## 💾 Step 4: Save Your Work

```bash
git add .
git commit -m "Created initial text files"
git push origin main
```

---

## 🔄 Step 5: Simulate Remote Update (git pull)

1. Open your repository on GitHub
2. Edit **tasks.txt** online and add another task
3. On your computer, run:

```bash
git pull origin main
```

Your local file now updates with the online change.

---

## ✏️ Step 6: Make More Local Changes

```bash
echo "Second note added locally" >> notes.txt
git add .
git commit -m "Added second note"
git push origin main
```

---

## 📁 Optional Step 7: Organize Files

```bash
mkdir archive
mv notes.txt ideas.txt archive/
git add .
git commit -m "Moved files into archive folder"
git push origin main
```

---

# ✅ Submission

Learners should submit:

* Screenshot of project folder structure
* Screenshot of terminal showing:

  * `git log`
  * `git status`
* A short reflection explaining:

  * How Git helped track versions
  * How `git pull` synced online updates

questions.question_text: What is the main purpose of Git?
options: To track file changes and manage versions
questions.explanation: Git is a version control system that tracks changes and allows you to manage different versions of your project.

questions.question_text: What is GitHub mainly used for?
questions.explanation: GitHub is a cloud platform that hosts Git repositories so they can be backed up and shared.

questions.question_text: Which command starts Git tracking in a project folder?
questions.explanation: 'git init' initializes a new Git repository in your project folder.

options: Stages all changed files for the next commit
questions.explanation: 'git add .' moves all modified files into the staging area so they can be committed.

options: A backup stored only on GitHub
A saved snapshot of your project at a specific time
questions.explanation: A commit records a snapshot of your project along with a message describing the changes.

questions.question_text: Which command shows the history of saved versions?
questions.explanation: 'git log' displays the commit history of the repository.

questions.question_text: What does 'git push origin main' do?
options: Uploads your committed changes to the main branch on GitHub
questions.explanation: 'git push origin main' sends your local commits to the 'main' branch of the remote repository.

questions.question_text: Why is 'origin' included in 'git push origin main'?
options: It is the name of your computer
It tells Git which remote repository to send changes to
It specifies the file being uploaded
questions.explanation: 'origin' is the default name for the remote repository (usually GitHub).

questions.question_text: What does 'git pull origin main' do?
options: Downloads and updates your local project with changes from GitHub
questions.explanation: 'git pull' fetches changes from the remote repository and merges them into your local branch.

questions.question_text: Which real-life example best describes Git?
options: A time machine that lets you go back to earlier versions of your work
questions.explanation: Git allows you to return to previous versions of your project, similar to a time machine.
